title: Marian Iconography Reference, the Visual Catechesis of Catholic Marian Devotion

sections.title: 1. Hodegetria (She Who Shows the Way)
sections.summary: arm. With her right hand she points to the child. The child, also frontal, holds a scroll (the Logos) or makes a gesture of blessing. Both gaze at the viewer rather than at each other.

sections.title: 2. Eleousa (She of Tender Mercy / Loving-Kindness)
sections.summary: child's small hand reaches up to caress his Mother's face or neck; she inclines her head toward him in maternal tenderness. The mood is intimate, sorrowful, contemplative.

sections.title: 3. Theotokos Panagia (the All-Holy Mother of God) / Orans
sections.summary: the orans (praying) gesture. Often a medallion of the Christ- child appears on her chest or in her womb, suggesting the Word-in-the-womb.

sections.title: 4. Platytera ton Ouranon (Wider Than the Heavens)
sections.summary: mandorla (a glory shape) showing the adult Christ within, often as a small Pantokrator. The visual paradox: the finite womb contains the infinite God.

sections.title: 5. Nikopoia (She Who Brings Victory)
sections.summary: seated directly on her lap (not on her arm), facing the viewer with the gesture of blessing or holding a scroll. The mood is

sections.title: Subordinate Eastern Types
sections.summary: child. The most maternal sub-type. Common in Coptic and Ethiopian iconography.

sections.title: 1. The Romanesque and Gothic Madonna (11th-13th c.)
sections.summary: frontally with the Christ-child on her lap, facing forward, often carved in wood. The faithful's eye is drawn to the Child enthroned on the Mother. Roughly equivalent to the Byzantine Nikopoia.

sections.title: 2. The Italian Renaissance Madonna (14th-16th c.)
sections.summary: The Madonna and Child as the central genre of Italian Renaissance painting. Naturalistic, humane, often set in landscape or domestic interior. The Renaissance Madonna domesticated the divine motherhood, making it accessible to lay piety.

sections.title: 3. The Northern Renaissance Madonna
sections.summary: of the small.

sections.title: 4. The Pietà
sections.summary: The dead Christ in the arms of his Mother, the Sixth Sorrow of Mary, the iconography of the Co-Redemptrix in the most concentrated form.

sections.title: 5. The Annunciation Iconography
sections.summary: The most-painted Marian scene in Western art after the Madonna and Child. The standard elements:

sections.title: 6. The Coronation of the Virgin
sections.summary: The iconographic culmination of the Assumption and Queenship.

sections.title: 7. The Immaculate Conception (the Inmaculada)
sections.summary: The dogmatic Marian iconography of post-Trent Spanish Catholicism. The standardized composition (codified by Francisco Pacheco, the father-in-law of Velázquez, in his Arte de la Pintura, 1649):

sections.title: 8. Our Lady of Sorrows (Mater Dolorosa)
sections.summary: one sword, often visible against the breast). Her face is grave; she may be tearful or composed.

sections.title: 9. The Sacred Heart of Mary (Immaculate Heart)
sections.summary: surrounded by roses or lilies.

sections.title: 10. Modern Devotional Holy-Card Tradition
sections.summary: The mass-produced Catholic Marian image of the 19th and 20th centuries, mostly French in origin (Lourdes, Miraculous Medal) and Italian (the Sacred Heart industry of post-Risorgimento Italy).

sections.title: Colors
sections.summary: contemplation, of fidelity, of immaculate purity. The expensive Renaissance pigment ultramarine (lapis lazuli) was reserved for Marian mantles because of its preciousness.

sections.title: Numerology
sections.summary: 12:1, the tribes of Israel and the twelve apostles.

sections.title: Spatial Composition
sections.summary: contemplative.

sections.title: In Rome
sections.summary: Byzantine-style Hodegetria, ancient (tradition: 6th c. or earlier; possibly painted by St. Luke, per pious tradition). Held in the Borghese Chapel of the Basilica of St. Mary Major. Visited regularly by Popes; Pope Francis has made this image central to his pontificate, visiting it before and after every apostolic journey.

sections.title: In Italy
sections.summary: Marian house tradition (Mary's house at Nazareth translated by angels to Loreto in 1294). The cult of Loreto gives its name to the Litany.

sections.title: In France
sections.summary: de la Belle Verrière* stained glass, 12th c.; the cathedral's veil of the Virgin relic). The supreme Marian Gothic cathedral.

sections.title: In Spain
sections.summary: Madonna, distinct from the Mexican Guadalupe but giving her her name).

sections.title: In Poland
sections.summary: Byzantine-style icon, traditionally attributed to St. Luke (with cosmetic scars on the cheek added by 15th c. iconoclasts; the scars preserved as part of the image's history). The icon was carried in defense of the monastery of Jasna Góra in the Swedish Deluge of 1655; afterwards, Mary was crowned Queen of Poland by King John II Casimir in 1656.

sections.title: In Russia / the East
sections.summary: Constantinopolitan Eleousa, brought to Russia, the most venerated icon in Russian Christianity. Carried in procession against Tamerlane in 1395 (whose army turned back).

sections.title: In the Americas
sections.summary: 1531. The single most-visited Catholic shrine in the world (over 20 million pilgrims annually).

sections.title: In Ireland, England, and the Old Catholic Britain
sections.summary: reproduction at the Apparition Chapel).

sections.title: Theotokos (Mother of God, Ephesus 431)
sections.summary: Mary holds Christ, the Theotokos is confessed.

sections.title: Perpetual Virginity
sections.summary: stars of perpetual virginity on her veil (forehead, both shoulders): virgo ante partum, in partu, post partum.

sections.title: Immaculate Conception (Pius IX 1854)
sections.summary: standing on the crescent moon (Rev 12:1), crowned with twelve stars, the serpent crushed beneath her feet (Gen 3:15), clothed in white and blue. Often the Litany of Loreto symbols in the surrounding sky.

sections.title: Assumption (Pius XII 1950)
sections.summary: or extended in supplication and adoration. Often the apostles are shown looking up from below (the tomb empty).

sections.title: Queenship (Ad Caeli Reginam 1954)
sections.summary: by the Trinity.

sections.title: Mediatrix of All Graces (taught magisterially, not yet defined)
sections.summary: with rays streaming from her hands toward the world (graces) and some rays dim (graces not asked for).

sections.title: Co-Redemptrix (taught magisterially, not yet defined)
sections.summary: Coronation alongside her crucified Son.

sections.title: A Note on Forbidden and Suspect Imagery
sections.summary: The Catholic tradition has rejected, by formal magisterial action or by long pastoral practice, certain Marian images that distort doctrine. Brief notes:
